design_philosophy.core_loop: Light gun shooting gallery: aim and shoot armed enemies while avoiding civilians and police officers across themed urban crime scenarios
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Accuracy under pressure - distinguish threat (masked/goggled criminals) from non-threat (barefaced civilians/officers) in real-time; weapon scarcity and point-scoring anxiety
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elite law enforcement operative cleaning up city crime with escalating firepower; rank progression through performance excellence
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Explicit moral scoring system (rank demotion for civilian casualties) embedded in a photorealistic violence framework; demands precision marksmanship and threat assessment rather than spray-and-pray; arcade cabinet-derived model prioritizes accessibility with revolver reload (aim off-screen) mechanics

difficulty.lives_system: Standard lives system; player can continue after game over at operator discretion (arcade) or explicit continue option (home versions)
difficulty.continues_system: Continue system available after losing all lives
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based checkpointing; completing a stage (defeating boss) is required to progress
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Arcade operator dip-switch setting allows choice between linear arcade mode (forced stage progression) and street mode (stage selection); home console versions inherit these options

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon pickup system with escalating lethality: revolver → .357 Magnum → semi-automatic pistol → combat shotgun → assault rifle → submachine gun (single-use) → grenade launcher (single-use). Weapon loss on player death reverts to revolver, creating resource tension
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage progression gated by boss defeat; arcade mode forces linear stage order; street mode allows stage selection after initial completion
replayability_hooks: Rank system (Patrolman to Commander) tied to civilian casualty rate - incentivizes no-kill civilian runs
Points-per-kill scoring system (8 points per enemy, 10 points per destructible object) rewards high-accuracy speedrunning
Bonus lives every 2,000 points encourage resource optimization
Two-player cooperative mode with simultaneous weaponless progression
Street mode stage selection enables non-linear challenge routing

world_building_techniques: Initial dispatcher radio call establishing crime wave premise (Chicago PD officer Don Marshall at donut shop)
Stage-themed locations (Bank Robbery, Chinatown/Downtown, Hijacking, Drug Dealers/Gunrunners, Chemical Plant) implying organized crime structure
Enemy visual coding (sunglasses, ski masks, gas masks) vs. civilian/officer identification (barefaced) reinforces law enforcement perspective
Boss encounters feature exposed antagonist faces (no civilians present during final confrontation) emphasizing individual criminal threat

boss_design.philosophy: Stage-culminating criminal kingpin encounters requiring precise elimination without civilian interference; each boss represents organizational hierarchy tier

faction_indicators: Major crime organization invading Chicago (unspecified criminal cartel structure)
Chicago Police Department elite officer unit (protagonist Don Marshall is one of two survivors; most hospitalized or deceased)
Organized crime hierarchy implied through staged boss encounters (bank robbers, drug dealers, gunrunners, chemical plant saboteurs)
danger_signals: Visual threat identification: masked/goggled enemies vs. bare-faced allies (binary friend-or-foe system)
Collateral damage penalty: civilian casualties result in rank demotion, establishing moral law enforcement framework
Weapon loss upon player death forces momentary vulnerability to basic revolver
environmental_storytelling: Stage locations reflect urban crime infrastructure: bank district, ethnic community (Chinatown), transportation hub (hijacking), distribution networks (drug/gun runners), industrial targets (chemical plant)
Boss encounters in civilian-free zones indicate criminal hideouts separate from public spaces
Weapon pickups found during progression suggest criminal armories and supply chains

sources_used: wikipedia: Lethal Enforcers (gameplay mechanics, weapons system, ranks, plot, ports)
tcrf: Lethal Enforcers (SNES) (developer/publisher attribution, technical debugging features)
